name: Kegare (Ritual Impurity)
shortDescription: Kegare refers to a state of ritual impurity or defilement in Shinto and Japanese folk beliefs, stemming from contact with death, disease, or other unclean elements.
description:
Kegare (穢れ) is a fundamental concept in Shinto and traditional Japanese culture, denoting a state of ritual impurity or defilement. It is not necessarily moral impurity but rather a condition that disrupts the harmony and purity of the sacred sphere. Kegare arises from contact with elements considered unclean or taboo, primarily death (especially contact with corpses), childbirth (considered polluting due to blood), menstruation, disease, and contact with bodily fluids. 

Kegare is believed to be contagious and can spread to objects, places, and people. It is seen as a disruption of the natural order and can bring misfortune. The presence of kegare necessitates purification rituals (harae) to restore balance and cleanse the affected person or place. These rituals are crucial for maintaining the purity of shrines and ensuring the efficacy of prayers and offerings. Shimenawa ropes, often seen at the entrances of shrines, serve to demarcate the sacred space and prevent kegare from entering. The concept of kegare is also related to the story of Izanagi's journey to Yomi (the underworld) and his subsequent purification ritual, highlighting the importance of separating the realms of the living and the dead. The concept is integral to understanding Shinto's emphasis on purity, cleanliness, and the avoidance of anything that might disrupt the sacred order. It also influences social customs and practices surrounding death, childbirth, and hygiene in Japan. The concept is also related to the symbolism surrounding Ama-no-Iwato, as the world was in a state of impurity and chaos before Amaterasu emerged.

sources.title: Living Kami: Five Studies of Shinto Spirituality
sources.author: Robert S. Ellwood
